Pilot / POC spike Verdict: PROCEED to Phase 3 Ceiling test · 2 families · time-boxed

Can an LLM-crafted requirements page clearly out-communicate the deterministic v2 render — enough to justify giving up render determinism?

Where it landed
Yes — by hand, for both tested families.
The quality bar is reachable, so the recommendation is to commit to the Phase-3 maker pipeline.
What this is (and isn't)

A deliberately cheap, hand-built probe of one bet — answered for two real families. Not a shipped feature, and not a proof that an unaided agent or all nine families clear the same bar.

The bet under test

The question this spike was probing

This was a deliberately cheap, time-boxed probe of a single bet — does giving up render determinism buy enough reader comprehension to be worth it — answered by hand, not shipped.

Before committing to a full Phase-3 maker pipeline, the spike set out to prove by hand that an LLM-crafted requirements page can clearly out-communicate the v2 deterministic render for at least two work families — or to surface, with evidence, that it cannot.

A human crafted maker pages for two real classified documents following the cast-preso-how discipline, then audited them against the deterministic baselines on three axes: canonical-id coverage, single-file self-containment, and above-the-fold scannability.

Crucially, this is a ceiling validation: it establishes that the bar exists and can be cleared by hand — not that an unaided agent will clear it. It is explicitly time-boxed — two families is the gate bar, and the stretch third family is descoped the moment budget runs thin. The deliverable is a recommended gate disposition plus the audit artifacts, handed to the owner at G1 for the binding maker-vs-hybrid call.

The result

Where the spike landed

PROCEED to Phase 3 — the quality bar (clearly beat the v2 page, carry every canonical id verbatim on the right block, stay a self-contained single file) was reachable by hand for both bug_fix and new_initiative.

Strong · structural win

bug_fix

The deterministic baseline silently drops five of seven canonical ids; the maker surfaces all seven in family-appropriate sections. A measured comprehension gap — not a taste call.

Qualified win

new_initiative

Id coverage at parity, but the maker keeps the whole WHAT open in under half the bytes where the baseline collapses depth behind thirteen <details> — a hierarchy-and-scannability win that still needs the human-eyeball confirmation.

Recorded anomaly — flagged, not acted on

The v2 success-criteria checker is necessary-but-not-sufficient: it passes the deterministic baseline too, so it cannot be the quality gate for a varying render. Logged as Phase-4a input.

The edge of the evidence

What this spike deliberately did not prove

Two cleared families is a ceiling proof — not a breadth proof and not an autonomy proof. Both unknowns are open, deferred, and re-validated agent-generated downstream.

  • Deferred · breadth

    Family breadth across all nine families is not proven by a two-family ceiling spike.

    Re-validated agent-generated in Phase 5 — out of scope here.
  • Deferred · autonomy

    Whether the eventual Phase-3 agent clears the bar unaided is not proven — the spike only proves the bar is reachable by hand.

    Phase 3 re-proves it agent-generated, reusing this spike's id-audit pattern.
Pilot / POC spike · family-pilot-poc · ceiling validation, two families, time-boxed
